title: Connecting a water butt to a greenhouse for gravity-fed irrigation
subtitle: Positioning, guttering, pipework, and a simple drip system
excerpt: A greenhouse roof of 6 square metres collects approximately 3,600 litres of rainwater in a UK average year. Connected to a water butt via a simple gutter and downpipe, this water can supply gravity-fed drip irrigation to pots and growbags, reducing mains water use during the growing season.
sourceNotes: RHS guidance on rainwater harvesting from greenhouse roofs. Irrigation design principles for gravity-fed drip systems. Graf and Harcostar water butt installation guides.

content.text: Most greenhouse designs (polycarbonate or glass, aluminium or timber frame) can accept a small eaves gutter. The roof area determines how much water can be collected: a 3 m x 2 m greenhouse (6 m2 roof) in a UK average rainfall of 600 mm per year collects approximately 3,600 litres. In practice, some rainfall is lost to evaporation and splash, but even in a dry summer a well-positioned butt refills several times.

Half-round or square plastic guttering in 76 mm or 100 mm profile is suitable. Fix brackets to the greenhouse eaves at 600 mm centres with a slight fall toward the downpipe end (5-10 mm per metre). The downpipe connects to the water butt via a diverter kit (fits into the downpipe and redirects water into the butt once it fills, overflowing back into the downpipe to drain away when the butt is full). A
is worth adding if the water will be used on edible crops.

Position the butt against the greenhouse wall below the downpipe. Raise it on a stable stand (see the linked tutorial) to at least 300 mm above the greenhouse floor level: this gives enough head pressure for gravity drip irrigation to reach all parts of the greenhouse. A 200-litre or 250-litre butt is the practical maximum for a small greenhouse; larger volumes may require a stronger stand.

Connect 13 mm or 16 mm poly pipe to the butt tap. Run along the greenhouse bench. Insert drip stakes (adjustable-flow emitter stakes, available from irrigation suppliers) into each pot or growbag. Open the tap to a low setting; adjust emitters so each pot receives a slow drip over 20-30 minutes. Test that flow continues throughout the session: flow drops as the butt empties because head pressure falls. Automate with a simple battery-powered tap timer if needed.
